rationale: The cited paper appears highly relevant to the citing paper’s core constructs: both focus on causal social interactions, asymmetry in influence, and managerial targeting implications (opinion-leader spillovers vs. in-flight social spillovers/homophily-feedback targeting). The citing note explicitly frames its contribution as extending prior social-effects identification work and challenging classical contagion assumptions, which makes this 2010 asymmetric peer-effects study a plausible foundational/comparison source rather than an incidental mention.
